issues.title: Unaddressed Feedback from Reviews
issues.author: jiceatscion
issues.body:
(Moved from https://github.com/scionassociation/standards/issues/64)

IETF 115 Feedback Russ on specific sections: 

- [x] Section 2.2.1.4.1 is incomplete.  You do not say which string type is associated with the ISD-AS number attribute.  I suggest that you keep it simple and use PrintableString.
_--> This feedback is addressed in the new version of the draft, in the section Certificate Specification > Basic Fields._ 
- [x] Section 2.2.2.1.2 is confusing to me.  I do not understand the need for id-kp-root.  Further, the "MUST be set" language is not clear, since the extension is a list of OIDs.  The phrase "MUST be included" might be a better way to talk about it.
_--> This feedback is addressed in the new version of the draft._ 
- [ ] #28. 

General feedback Eliot:
- [x] Make sure the references are correct:
       - Normative references: Needed for the specification; specification is based on it.
       - Informative references: Provide background/additional information
- [x] Make sure MUST/MAY/SHOULD are used correctly (see also [RFC 8174](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8174)): 
       - MUST: Without this, the protocol will not work
       - SHOULD: Should do this unless you have a very good reason not to
       - MAY: Try to not use "MAY", unless not needed
- [ ] What text can be removed? Be concise. Make sure every word that is there must be there.

> A trust reset is only required in the case the number of	
 	      compromised keys at the same time is greater or equal than the	
 	      TRC's quorum (see Section 3.1.2.2.7).

issues.title: Clarify relationship to RPKI
issues.author: nicorusti
issues.body:
Add a couple of sentences to clarify relationship to RPKI

>Date: 	Tue, 6 Aug 2024 23:44:57 +0000
From: 	Michael McBride [<michael.mcbride@futurewei.com>](mailto:michael.mcbride@futurewei.com)
To: 	[rfc-ise@rfc-editor.org](mailto:rfc-ise@rfc-editor.org) [<rfc-ise@rfc-editor.org>](mailto:rfc-ise@rfc-editor.org)
CC: 	'Mike McBride' [<mmcbride7@gmail.com>](mailto:mmcbride7@gmail.com)F. How else can the document be improved?
 
> I’m a believer. It’s a good set of solutions. Using a PKI is a good solution. As is the current RPKI and ROA’s. It might also be helpful, in the scion control plan pki document, to at least acknowledge RPKI. RPKI is widely used now and it could be helpful to understand how scions use of PKI would be similar and different. In the long run it would give operators a more comfortable understanding since most now use RPKI.  
We could add a couple of sentences about this. We have some text in the now expired [draft-rustignoli-panrg-scion-components](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-rustignoli-panrg-scion-components-03.t): 



We have some text in the now expired [draft-rustignoli-panrg-scion-components](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-rustignoli-panrg-scion-components-03.t): 
```
   The above-mentioned decoupling also implies that SCION does not
   provide, by design, IP prefix origin validation, which is currently
   provided by RPKI [RFC8210].  As prefix origin validation is outside
   of SCION's scope, IP-to-SCION's coexistence mechanisms (SIAM, SBAS)
   later discussed in Section 3.1 build on top of RPKI for IP origin
   attestation.
   ... 
   These properties would be lost if SCION were to rely on an existing
   PKI (i.e., the web PKI, the RPKI, ...).  For example, if SCION were
   to use the RPKI instead of the CP-PKI, its control plane would lack
   the trust model required to support Isolation Domains.  This is
   because RPKI's trust model follows the same structure as the IP
   allocation hierarchy, where the five RIRs represent the trust roots.
```

comments.author: knmeynell
comments.body: Added reference to why RPKI isn't directly supported by SCION. Did not mention SIAM or SBAS as this appears to be experimental and would need to be further explained somewhere.

comments.author: nicorusti

@oncilla wrote on [Slack](https://scionproto.slack.com/archives/C8ADA9CEP/p1740057971050929?thread_ts=1740057439.410749&cid=C8ADA9CEP)
> Q: MUST a CA have its own AS? 
A: Yes. A CA must have an ISD-AS number assigned, and it must be present in the certificate 

This is because `2.2.1.2.1.  ISD-AS number Attribute` is part of the cert. We shoudl clarify in the text that this is the case and why (e.g. is the AS field in a cert used by the CP to request that cert?)

> Q: Is there a 1:1 relationship between CP root and CA certificates? In other words, MUST every CA certificate have a corresponding Root certificate in the TRC?
A: In practice this is the case, and also the intention. I don't think it is currently enforced. I would also not prescribe it in the spec. This feels like something that the ISD governance should decide

comments.author: nicorusti
comments.body:
Observation from Ramon Keller related to this issue (libc):

See "Section 4.3"

According to the current definition, only the AS certificate is returned by the CA. However, given that the Control Place CA certificate is renewed approximately every 7–8 days, a new Control Place CA certificate can be expected with roughly every third AS certificate renewal. The draft does not specify how the updated CA certificate is to be disseminated, leaving this behavior to the implementation.

Returning the Control Place CA certificate together with the CP AS certificate is one solution. The other option would be to provide and support the AIA extension in the certificates, but AIA is certainly not the most efficient solution.

This is already described in the CP draft in  Figure 30: Control Service gRPC API - Trust Material representation.

issues.title: PKI scalability
issues.author: nicorusti
issues.body:
DO we need to mention something about PKI scalability?

#### Feedback Alvaro on CP: 
```
...
2060	3.3.1.  Intra-ISD Beaconing
...
2100	   If the same AS has 1000 child links, the propagation of the beacons
2101	   will require signing one new AS entry for each of the propagated PCBs
2102	   for each link (at most 50 per link), i.e. at most 50000 signatures
2103	   per propagation event.  The total bandwidth for the propagation of
2104	   these PCBs for all 1000 child links would, be roughly around 25 MB/s
2105	   which is manageable with even modest consumer hardware.
```
[] Yes, the bw is manageable.  The signature verification may be the bottleneck (considering similar behavior in BGPSec), is that correct?

comments.author: nicorusti
comments.body:
Observation from Ramon Keller (libc):

See "Section 3.1 Trust Model"

Each ISD has to implement more than one CP-PKI to adhere to the goal of having no single point of failure in the control plane. Smaller ISDs therefore have to bear high operational costs if they have to operate several PKIs on their own.

In order to reduce costs, it must be possible and explicitly desired that trust centres that operate CP-PKIs can offer their services to multiple ISDs. (Multi-tenant wise.)

comments.author: nicorusti
comments.body: TODO: add a sentence like "signing PCBs might become a bottleneck from a computation point of view, therefore it is recommended to deploy multiple PKI instances.". This could  CP draft in `3.3.1.  Intra-ISD Beaconing` or maybe the PKI draft.

comments.author: nicorusti
comments.body:
We already added this wording in the CP draft: 
> For much  larger, more highly connected ASes, the path discovery tasks of the
   Control Service can be distributed over many instances in order to
   increase the PCB throughput.

issues.title: Self-Signed Voting Certificates
issues.author: knmeynell
issues.body:
Observation from Ramon Keller (libc):

See "Section 1.5 Overview of Certificates, Keys and Roles"

Self-Signed certificates just add some identity information to a public key for easier identification. They do not increase security unless their public key is verified explicitly upon each use because an attacker can easily falsify any self-signed certificate with every detail of the original, including the signature applied with the fake private key. In this section, a cross reference to the TRC Ceremony could be added, where the public keys of the voting certificates must explicitly be verified.

issues.title: SCION Certificate Specification deviates from permitted data types for individual RDN attributes
issues.author: knmeynell
issues.body:
Observation from Ramon Keller (libc):

See "2.2.1. Basic Fields: SCION-Specific Constraints and Conditions"

Issuer and Subject DN - The SCION Certificate Specification Standard is based on [RFC5280] and [X.509], but deviates from the permitted data types for individual RDN attributes. While the general X.509 standard, as described in ITU-T X.520, prescribes the data type PrintableString for countryName and serialNumber, SCION generally requires UTF-8 coding. Such contradictions to the standard can lead to complications in various contexts, such as directory services and their schemas.

Validity - The draft states that GeneralizedTime is not permitted and must be rejected, allowing only UTCTime. However, this appears to conflict with RFC 5280, which mandates the use of GeneralizedTime for dates on or after the year 2050.

issues.title: Signing TRCs using EKU does not align with semantics of timestamping
issues.author: knmeynell
issues.body:
Observation from Ramon Keller (libc):

See "2.2.2 Extensions"

The Root CA certificates must contain the timestamping OID in its extended key usage extension but must not set the digitalSignature flag in the key usage extension. It is understood that the root CA certificate must include an additional key usage beyond keyCertSign to support TRC signing, but requirement to include the Extended Key Usage (EKU) OID for timestamping raises questions.

According to RFC 5280, the EKU extension is intended for End Entity certificates, and the act of signing TRCs does not align with the original semantics of timestamping. It is unclear why this functionality is not based on the digitalSignature key usage alone.

issues.title: Mandating that certificates be retrieved from TRC payload limits interoperability with existing CMS libraries
issues.author: knmeynell
issues.body:
Observation from Ramon Keller (libc):

See "Section 3.1.3.1"
```
   *  SignedData sequence:

      -  The certificates field MUST be left empty.  The certificate
         pool used to verify a TRC update is already specified in the
         certificates field of the predecessor TRC's payload (see also
         Section 3.1.2.2.11).
```

Requiring the Certificates field in the CMS `SignedData` structure to be empty, and instead mandating that certificates be retrieved from the Certificates field of the TRC payload, significantly limits interoperability with existing CMS libraries. Most CMS implementations expect the signing certificate to be present within the Certificates field of the SignedData structure. As a result, signature verification logic would need to be re-implemented, or the parsed CMS structure would need to be modified by injecting the certificates from the TRC payload into the Certificates field—despite this field not being part of the signed data—solely to satisfy library expectations.

issues.title: Creation of a new AS certificate
issues.author: knmeynell

Observation from Ramon Keller (libc):

See "Section 4.3"

According to the current definition, only the AS certificate is returned by the CA. However, given that the Control Place CA certificate is renewed approximately every 7–8 days, a new Control Place CA certificate can be expected with roughly every third AS certificate renewal. The draft does not specify how the updated CA certificate is to be disseminated, leaving this behavior to the implementation.

Returning the Control Place CA certificate together with the CP AS certificate is one solution. The other option would be to provide and support the AIA extension in the certificates, but AIA is certainly not the most efficient solution.

issues.title: Scalability and cost effectiveness
issues.author: knmeynell
issues.body:
Observation from Ramon Keller (libc):

See "Section 3.1 Trust Model"

Each ISD has to implement more than one CP-PKI to adhere to the goal of having no single point of failure in the control plane. Smaller ISDs therefore have to bear high operational costs if they have to operate several PKIs on their own.

In order to reduce costs, it must be possible and explicitly desired that trust centres that operate CP-PKIs can offer their services to multiple ISDs. (Multi-tenant wise.)

issues.title: Substitution to Certificate Revocation
issues.author: knmeynell
issues.body:
Observation from Ramon Keller (libc):

See "Section 1.4.2"

Certificate revocation is described as unreliable and compensated for with the concept of short-lived certificates. Instead of withdrawing certificates that are no longer trustworthy, certificates that are still valid are reissued again and again.

With a certificate validity period of three days and a grace period of approx. 1.5 days, the entire stock of valid certificates is renewed every 1.5 days. This places extremely high demands on the reliability of a CP-PKI and therefore also a high load on the CP-PKI systems. Although no comparison to loads on streaming portals, this concept is certainly not energy-efficient.

This load increases linearly with the growth of ASes within an ISD, and it will probably be the large, general-purpose ISDs that are orientated towards a lower trust level and cannot afford the financial or human resources to operate a highly available CP-PKI. Could the criticality of CP-PKI become a disabler of SCION technology?

SCION delegates authorization to the CP-PKI, but certificates should only be used to authenticate. Currently, whoever possesses an AS Certificate is enabled to communicate but this keeps the discussed attack window open.

Does SCION implement other mechanisms to exclude malicious participants? Something like a PCB-blocking, decided and issued by the core ASes as such an incident is reported to them? What are the consequences to leaf nodes attached to any blocked / not re-certified node in general?

comments.author: nicorusti
comments.body: AFAIK there are no other mechanisms implemented. I'll resolve this and save this as a consideration for future work

comments.author: nicorusti
comments.body: P.S. Based on the discussion in our September TC, we already explain why we don't have revocation in paragraph ### Substitutes to Certificate Revocation .

issues.title: CA certificates - evaluate use of link certificates
issues.author: knmeynell
issues.body:
Observation from Ramon Keller (libc):

See "Sections 2.1.2, 2.1.3 & 2.1.4"

Root Certificates (added to the TRC) are recommended to be valid for just one year only. This requires that a Root CA is replaced by its successor every year in a TRC ceremony. (Sensitive TRC update).

Would it be conceivable to introduce the concept of link certificates to transfer the trust from one root CA to another in order to reduce the effort involved in sensitive TRC updates?

Concept taken from ICAO Doc 9303 part12:

A specific link certificate is signed by the Root CA key which is to be decommissioned. The public key contained within the link certificate is the one of the new root key. The link certificate technically looks like a certificate issued to a sub-CA, but it is used differently. It has a shorter lifetime and must not be used to sign any other certificates. It is solely used to transfer the trust from the old root key to the new key. The old key confirms the rise of the new one.

The current strategy to let the CA sign the new TRC with the old CA key also works, but it requires a TRC update. On the other hand, link certificates would not require a TRC update, but the clients would need to maintain the list of link certificates from the root in the TRC down to the current CA certificate which also introduces some complexity.

comments.author: nicorusti
comments.body: This is for future protocol evolution. It was discussed with the TC but it won't be possible to add this into the draft, which discusses the current implementation.

issues.title: Operational Considerations
issues.author: knmeynell
issues.body:
Observations from Ramon Keller (libc):

**AS Certificate creation:**

Initial AS certificates are still described as to be submitted out of band. Is there any progress of the process in this respect since the SSFN implementation?

**Short certificate lifetime:**

The SCION draft still recommends very short lifetimes for Root CA and Issuing CA certificates of 1 year or 11 days (!), respectively. Renewal in such short lifetimes will kill any operations team running certain commercial products. These products are sold in conjunction with HSM and much longer certificate lifetimes than those that were e.g. implemented for SSFN. Anybody wanting to implement the recommended lifetimes needs to look for a capable solution.

**Control Plane AS Certificates:**

The recommended validity of 3 days is fair, but then the initialization processes must be stable and effective. Could SIX provide any experience based feedback on how much the end users relied on a longer duration for initial AS certificates when onboarding to SSFN?

**Processes**

The draft talks a lot about the technical elements of a CP-PKI, but not much about processes the PKI operators have to implement. or the regulations.

**Cost of Ownership**

The range extends from simple technical systems focussing on stable network communication at affordable costs to highly secure environments that place great value on reliable network communication in combination with a high level of data protection. Those with higher security requirements should be willing to bear the higher security costs through secure processes, but only those with technical requirements should be exempt from additional costs as far as possible.

**High availability in CAs**

1. Must the CAs of an ISD offer backup CA services to all the other PKIs in the ISD?
2. What if a single PKI fails to operate?
3. To cope with this single point of failure, it should be specified that the subscribers could quickly switch over to one of the other PKIs to be able to renew their certificates on time.
4. How is the subscriber database synchronized among the different backup CAs, as without a specification the SCION world will face incompatible implementation-specific approaches.

The current BCP on this is [RFC7696](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7696#section-2.2). It says:

   of one document without necessarily requiring an update to the other.

issues.title: Brian Trammell's PKI review
issues.author: knmeynell
issues.body:
Brian Trammell's comments...

The SCION Control Plane PKI draft (PKI; draft-dekater-scion-pki-10) defines the trust architecture that underpins the entire SCION system. It establishes a framework for authenticating control plane messages and path information through a novel, domain-based trust model. It specifies the cryptographic underpinnings constituting ISDs and the trust relationships within them. A Trust Root Configuration (TRC) acts as the trust anchor for an ISD, containing the root certificates needed to validate a hierarchy of CA and AS certificates, as well as the policies for its own evolution through regular and sensitive updates.

This approach provides what the document calls "trust agility," allowing different parts of the network to operate under distinct, self-contained trust environments, a significant departure from the global, monolithic trust models of the web PKI. Instead of traditional certificate revocation lists, the CP-PKI relies on relatively short-lived certificates and TRC updates to manage compromised keys. The PKI is the foundation upon which the SCION Control Plane (CP) builds its secure beaconing and path discovery mechanisms, and by extension, provides the trust necessary for the Data Plane (DP) to forward packets along authenticated paths.

The document is well put together (aside from a few nits) and explains the functionality of the PKI well. The questions I have mainly concern the operational consequences of the certificate management periods at each level of the hierarchy down from the TRC: the grace and overlap periods seem from first principles to be sufficient to manage availability risk due to higher-frequency key rotation, but it would be interesting to see how this plays out in reality (I can't really think of a way to analyze this except in production, or with a questionably-applicable simulation).

Further "out there", I'd also be interested in exploring how the PKI itself could be incrementally applied to routing security with the existing interdomain control plane (this idea is inchoate enough that I can't really seriously elaborate it further in the context of a review).

_KM - I think these are philosophical questions._

issues.title: 3.1.4.1. TRC Selection For Trust Anchor Pool -> do we need python pseudo-code?
issues.author: nicorusti
issues.body:
Can we do something better than having all this code? 

```
~~~~python
    def select_trust_anchors(trcs: Dict[(int,int), TRC], \
    verification_time: int) -> Set[RootCert]:
        """
        Args:
            trcs: The dictionary mapping (serial number, \
            base number) to the TRC for a given ISD.
            verification_time: The time of verification.

        Returns:
            The set of CP Root certificates acting as trust anchors.
        """
        # Find highest base number that has a TRC with validity
        # period starting before verification time.
        base_nr = 1
        for trc in trcs.values()
            if trc.id.base_nr > base_nr and trc.validity.not_before \
            <= verification_time:
                base_nr = trc.id.base_nr

        # Find TRC with highest serial number with given base number
        # and a validity period starting before verification time.
        serial_nr = 1
        for trc in trcs[isd].values():
            if trc.id.base_nr != base_nr:
                continue
            if trc.id.serial_nr > serial_nr and \
            trc.validity.not_before <= verification_time:
                serial_nr = trc.id.serial_nr

        candidate = trcs[(serial_nr, base_nr)]

        # If the verification time is not inside the validity period,
        # there is no valid set of trust anchors.
        if not candidate.validity.contains(verification_time):
            return set()

        # If the grace period has passed, only the certificates in
        # that TRC may be used as trust anchors.
        if candidate.validity.not_before + candidate.grace_period \
        < verification_time:
            return collect_trust_anchors(candidate)

        predecessor = trcs.get((serial_nr-1, base_nr))
        if not predecessor or predecessor.validity.not_after < \
        verification_time:
            return collect_trust_anchors(candidate)

        return collect_trust_anchors(candidate) | \
        collect_trust_anchors(predecessor)


    def collect_trust_anchors(trc: TRC) -> Set[RootCert]:
        """
        Args:
            trc: A TRC from which the CP Root Certificates shall \
            be extracted.

        Returns:
            The set of CP Root certificates acting as trust anchors.
        """
        roots = set()
        for cert in trc.certificates:
            if not cert.basic_constraints.ca:
                continue
            roots.add(cert)
        return roots
```

comments.author: nicorusti
comments.body:
e.g. Gemini proposes: 
```
### Trust Anchor Selection Algorithm

The selection of the right set of TRCs to build the trust anchor pool depends on the verification time. For control plane messages, the verification time is typically the current time. For auditing purposes, the verification time is the historical point in time when the signature was created.

To construct the trust anchor pool for a specific ISD at a given `verification_time`, a relying party MUST execute the following steps:

1. **Determine the Active Base Number:**
   Filter the set of all available TRCs for the ISD to those whose `notBefore` validity date is less than or equal to the `verification_time`. From this filtered set, select the highest `baseNumber`.

2. **Identify the Candidate TRC:**
   Filter the set of available TRCs again to find only those that match the `baseNumber` identified in Step 1, and whose `notBefore` date is less than or equal to the `verification_time`. From this set, select the TRC with the highest `serialNumber`. This is the *Candidate TRC*.

3. **Verify Candidate TRC Expiration:**
   If the `verification_time` is strictly greater than the Candidate TRC's `notAfter` date, the algorithm terminates and returns an empty set. There are no valid trust anchors for this time.

4. **Extract Candidate Trust Anchors:**
   Parse the `certificates` sequence of the Candidate TRC. Extract all certificates that possess a `basicConstraints` extension with the `cA` boolean asserted. Add these CP Root Certificates to the trust anchor pool.

5. **Evaluate the Grace Period:**
   Check if the Candidate TRC is currently within its grace period (i.e., `verification_time` <= `notBefore` + `gracePeriod`). If the grace period has expired, the algorithm terminates and returns the current trust anchor pool.

6. **Process the Predecessor TRC (If Applicable):**
   If the Candidate TRC is still within its grace period, attempt to retrieve the Predecessor TRC (the TRC with the same `baseNumber` and a `serialNumber` exactly one less than the Candidate TRC). 
   If the Predecessor TRC exists and its `notAfter` date is greater than or equal to the `verification_time`, extract its CP Root Certificates (as described in Step 4) and add them to the trust anchor pool.

7. **Return the Pool:**
   The algorithm terminates and returns the assembled trust anchor pool.
```

issues.title: Feedback P. Ambord
issues.author: nicorusti
issues.body:
Section 1.3
"The TRC also provides trust agility - that is it which enables users to select the trust roots used to initiate certificate validation."
The wording could be improved for clarity. The phrase "that is it which enables" reads awkwardly.

Section 1.4
"Each TRC consists of a collection of signed root CA certificates which are used to sign issuing CA certificates, which are in turn used to sign AS certificates."
I suggest using the terms "Root CA certificates" and "Issuing CA certificates" consistently. Both are CA certificates, and making that naming explicit would improve precision.

Section 1.4.2
"In the Control Plane PKI the number of certificates that each CA must renew is manageable as it is limited to at most the number of ASes within an ISD."
This statement may be too strict. In practice, there can be more certificates than ASes, for example where an AS has multiple gateways.
It may also be worth mentioning that the absence of CRL/OCSP checking improves performance by removing additional network lookups during PKI processing.

Section 2.1.5
"Voting certificates are X.509-style certificates."
This sounds vague compared to other parts of the text that refer more precisely to X.509 v3.
"Both SCION Control Plane root certificates and Control Plane CA certificates are in fact CA certificates. That is, they can both be used to verify other certificates."
This formulation appears imprecise. A Control Plane CA certificate cannot be used to validate another Control Plane CA certificate. However, both are involved in validating an AS certificate.
"The SCION root private key and public key/certificate are used to sign and verify the Control Plane CA certificates, respectively."
Only the certificate is needed for verification, since it contains the public key.
"The control plane CA certificates are explicitly NOT part of the TRC, for reasons of security."
I am not sure "for reasons of security" is the right characterization here. This seems more related to practicality or availability than to security in the strict sense.

Section 2.1.6
"Arrows show how signatures are verified; in other words, they indicate that a public key contained in a certificate or TRC can be used to verify the authenticity of another item."
The TRC contains certificates, not standalone public keys. That wording should be adjusted.

Section 3.1.3.1
"SCION implementations MUST fulfill the following additional rules, as well as the general syntax rules specified in [RFC5652]:"
There appears to be a typo in "fulfill".

Section 4.2.2
"A certificate chain is verified against the Control Plane root certificate, although the the root certificate is bundled with the TRC and not in the chain."
There is a duplicated "the".

issues.title: Language tags in TRC description (RFC 5198) and UTF-8 normalization
issues.author: nicorusti
issues.body:
From Alexey Melnikov: 

3.2.10. description

    The description field contains a UTF-8 encoded string that describes
    the ISD. It SHOULD NOT be empty. The description MUST be in English
    and MAY additionally contain information in other languages.

If information in other languages is conveyed, it should be tagged with language tags in some form, see RFC 5646.

Also, if one suggests using UTF-8 for human readable text, one should generally use (reference) something like RFC 5198.

comments.author: nicorusti
comments.body:
Key questions here are two: 
1.  Do we want to prescribe that UTF-8 text MUST be normalized according to [RFC5198](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5198.html#section-3)? 
This essentially ensures that text appearing identical to the human eye is also identical on a byte level, since UTF-8 has several identical looking characters. This could be important for crypto operations, but I'm not sure we ever use the description field for anything.   According to gemini, in scionproto we use `encoding/asn1` which does not normalize text according to RFC5198. 
2. Do we want to encourage (SHOULD) use of language tags according to RFC5646 ?  We don't use them.
Older versions of the SSFN TRC did not use language tags and were NOT in English, ut text was already UTF-8 encoded: 
e.g.
SSHN has various languages, but without tags:
```
“Die ISD 72 bildet die Grundlage für den HVR, den HIN Vertrauensraum. Der HVR ist ein für den Gesundheitsmarkt Schweiz bereitgestelltes, eigenständiges Kommunikationsnetzwerk, welches Gesundheitsfachpersonen, administrativen und technischen Hilfspersonen und deren Einrichtungen, medizinische und administrative Applikations- / Serviceanbieter /-Provider des Gesundheits- und Sozialwesens sowie Kostenträgern (“HVR Nutzer”) erlaubt, unter Reduktion der Cyberrisiken des Internet in einem geschützten System sicher untereinander zu kommunizieren, ohne dass diese bei demselben Telekommunikationsanbieter angeschlossen sein müssen. L'ISD 72 constitue la base du HVR, l’Espace de Confiance HIN. Le HVR est un réseau de communication autonome mis à la disposition du marché suisse de la santé. Il permet aux professionnels de la santé, aux auxiliaires administratifs et techniques et à leurs institutions, aux fournisseurs d'applications / de services / d'administration médicaux et sociaux ainsi qu'aux organismes payeurs ("utilisateurs de l’HVR") de communiquer entre eux de manière sécurisée dans un système protégé, en réduisant les cyber-risques d'Internet, sans devoir être raccordés au même fournisseur de télécommunications. L'ISD 72 costituisce la base della HVR, lo spazio della fiducia HIN. L’HVR è una rete di comunicazione indipendente messa a disposizione del mercato sanitario svizzero, che consente agli operatori sanitari, al personale amministrativo e di supporto tecnico e alle loro strutture, ai fornitori di applicazioni/servizi medici e amministrativi del settore sanitario e sociale, nonché ai sostenitori dei costi ("utenti HVR") di comunicare in modo sicuro tra loro in un sistema protetto, riducendo i rischi informatici di Internet, senza dover essere collegati allo stesso fornitore di telecomunicazioni. ISD 72 forms the basis of the HVR, the HIN Trust Space. The HVR is a stand-alone communication network provided for the Swiss healthcare market, which allows healthcare professionals, administrative and technical support staff and their facilities, medical and administrative application/service providers/providers of the healthcare and social sector as well as payers ("HVR Users") to communicate securely with each other in a protected system while reducing the cyber risks of the Internet, without having to be connected to the same telecommunications provider. ”
```


P.S. scionproto also seems to mention UTF-8 encoding [here](https://github.com/scionproto/scion/blob/368ef5973c7909d126902dc626c0530a0573e71d/pkg/scrypto/cppki/trc_asn1.go#L50), but I'm not sure if this is actually used:

comments.body: Maliciously change -> "Maliciously changing" or "To maliciously change".
"causing a kill switch" -> "Activating a kill switch"?

This last paragraph seems to make the scope a bit fuzzy. We should make it clear that this is only  talking about the CP PKI infrastructure here.
I'd suggest to replace this second paragraph, by concluding the thought from the paragraph above; if the control plane message came through, then the CP-PKI message will likely also come through. Ideally, we'd end by referring to the CP draft for more detailed discussion of DoS vulnerabilities of the different CP messages/rpcs. In the CP draft there is currently no discussion of DoS to the CP though.

I don't know if "kill switch" is an established technical term, but it sounds a bit vague to me. I think we should limit the scope to the CP-PKI and try to explain more precisely what type of kill switch this is referring to. Here's a try:

"In a public key infrastructures, the certificate authorities have both the responsibility and power to issue and revoke certificates. If a CA is compromised or malicious, this power can unavoidably be abused. A misbehaving CA can plainly refuse to issue certificates to legitimate entities, or even issue illegitimate certificates to allow impersonating another entity. In the context of the SCION control plane PKI, refusing an AS to obtain or renew their AS certificate will ultimately cut the AS off from the network, turning the CP-PKI into a network "kill switch". [SCION's trust architecture differs ....]"

>  Remote ISD/AS Kill Switch

I think this section / paragraph is redundant. It's already discussed in the introduction of the section that ISDs are isolated.

>Even if private keys became available to a remote attacker, they would need access to an AS inside the remote ISD to inject faulty information.

I don't think that's accurate. There are many ways to bring illegitimately signed AS entries into "circulation".
Both of these attacks (stop propagating / filter paths) would seem to fit better in the CP section.

For certificate renewals, the statement above does not apply (because they are not triggered a CP message).

I think DoS on the certificate renewal infrastructure might need to be discussed explicitly.

I think this would be clearer if we separately discuss compromise of every level of the CP-PKI hierarchy.
The distinction of core/non-core _AS_ is not entirely accurate; the distinction should be whether the AS keys, CA keys, root keys or voting keys have been compromised.

Again, I think we should focus on the CP-PKI here. We can replace the examples for "acting maliciously" to, "e.g. by not issuing or renewing certificates for certain ASes". 

Reading this though, it's not clear that a core AS acting in such a way can even be considered malicious. It's the job of the core AS to enforce an ISDs policy. So the message is more "If your not happy with an ISDs policy, then don't join this ISD". This seems rather self-evident, to be honest, and doesn't really fit in these security considerations. Maybe we can leave this paragraph away.

```suggestion
In a public key infrastructure, the certificate authorities have both the responsibility and power to issue and revoke certificates. If a CA is compromised or malicious, this power can be abused. A misbehaving CA could refuse to issue certificates to legitimate entities, or even issue illegitimate certificates to allow impersonation of another entity. In the context of the SCION control plane PKI, refusing to issue or renew a certificate to an AS will ultimately cut that AS off from the network, turning the CP-PKI into a network "kill switch".
```
Would something like 'Service Denial' be better than 'Kill Switch' throughout this section?

```suggestion
SCION’s trust architecture fundamentally differs from a global monopolistic trust model. In SCION, each ISD manages its own trust roots instead of a single global entity providing those roots. This structure gives each ISD autonomy in terms of key management and in terms of trust, and prevents the occurrence of a global kill switch affecting all ISDs at once. However, local ISD kill switches are still possible in SCION. The following sections explain these cases and possible countermeasures.
```

```suggestion
Compared to DNSSEC and BGPsec, a parent authority cannot switch off SCION core ASes as they manage their own cryptographic trust roots, but executing a kill switch *inside* a local ISD can be done at different levels of the AS-level hierarchy:
```

```suggestion
- On TRC level: The private root keys of the root certificates contained in an TRC are used to sign the CA certificates. If one of these private root keys is compromised, the adversary could issue illegitimate CA certificates which may be used in further attacks. To maliciously change the entire TRC through a TRC update is more complicated as each TRC update requires multiple different voting keys. The number of compromised voting keys needed to perform a malicious update of a TRC depends on the voting quorum set in the TRC: the higher the quorum, the more complicated malicious updating of a TRC will be.
```

```suggestion
If a core AS has not been compromised, but is instead acting maliciously (e.g., by not issuing or renewing certificates for certain ASes), one way to recover is for downstream ASes to self-organize and form a new ISD. By now operating autonomously, the new ISD can begin path discovery and traffic forwarding. SCION, unlike BGP, has no notion of routing convergence and instead the flooding of PCBs disseminates topology information. This means that in the worst case if all paths have to be re-created, fresh paths are established after a single flood has reached all ASes.
```

```suggestion
**Note** that in the above case, it may not be clear whether the core AS acting as described is actually behaving maliciously. It is the job of the core AS to enforce an ISD's policy. If a non-core AS within an ISD does not agree with ostensibly legal behavior of the core ASes, it is up to the non-core AS to stay within the ISD. Alternatively, the non-core AS could move to another ISD that suits its needs better.
```

```suggestion
If an AS realizes that it is missing any certificates, or that its certificates are no longer up-to-date (e.g., in case of certificate renewals and revocations), it must query the originator of the message for these. The same goes for an update of a TRC as updated TRCs are not distributed actively, but must be discovered by the ASes within the ISD. This happens via the processes of beaconing and path-lookup (see also [](#discover-trcupdate)). Upon discovery of a new TRC, an AS must ask the sender of the message including the new TRC number for this new TRC.
```

```suggestion
The above implies that the AS can *reach* the originating AS of the message, that is, that paths to the originator are available and that the relevant services at the originating AS are accessible. Denial of Service (DoS) attacks, where attackers overload different parts of the IT infrastructure, may impede this process of retrieving missing cryptographic material. However, the entire process of discovering and requesting missing cryptographical information is based on *control-plane* messages and communication. We therefore refer to {{I-D.scion-cp}} for a more detailed description of DoS vulnerabilities of control-plane messages.
```

However there are multiple CAs typically in an ISD, so this is not really a SPOF.  Text needs further adjustment to mention this. 
For example, in productive deployments, there are at least two independent CAs.
We are hinting to RPKI and DNSSEC, but without mentioning it. Maybe we could politely add a mention?

Right? I'm not fan of the wording here. As far as I understand, compromise of a root certificate means compromise of one of the CAs. Maybe we could reword the paragraph to mention this? 

Also, in principle a CA has  both a root certificate and a CA certificate (according to [here](https://docs.scion.org/en/latest/cryptography/certificates.html#cp-root-certificate), right? Are there cases where a CA certificate is issued to a different organization?

```suggestion
### Recovery from compromise
```
Once again, are "root" and CA AS the same? We should clarify this

I'd reword this section to just mention that the PKI relies on the CP's availability to disseminate certs, and that there are exceptions in case of certificate renewals, as discussed in an older discussion here https://github.com/scionassociation/scion-cppki_I-D/pull/25#discussion_r1418985770
Discussed above https://github.com/scionassociation/scion-cppki_I-D/pull/25#discussion_r1422657515

I and @knmeynell are also not sure about the term "Kill Switch". It is used sometimes at IETF, but not so much in RFCs. 
Maybe we can use something like single point of failure?

> Reading this though, it's not clear that a core AS acting in such a way can even be considered malicious. 

I agree, maybe we should remove that paragraph.
It would be good to mention that the CA certificates are not revoked, but short lived.
In the end I moved away from "kill switches" towards key compromise in https://github.com/scionassociation/scion-cppki_I-D/pull/25/commits/6658f2b31a57ad909146eabad82c8b1d59db56ac
This is mentioned a bit below. Resolving
Added note on multiple CAs in https://github.com/scionassociation/scion-cppki_I-D/pull/25/commits/1a562585fc21a86b0016e22d16f9c8e2c6deafe2
Removed in https://github.com/scionassociation/scion-cppki_I-D/pull/25/commits/6cb1402685fabf6c33241cfb70c065eb203379b2

@matzf is a trust reset only needed if the number of compromised >= quorum? If so, I'd change the text as follows: 
```suggestion
- On TRC level: If any of the root keys or voting keys contained in the TRC are compromised, the TRC must be updated as described in [](#update). Note that this is a sensitive TRC update, as the certificate related to the compromised private key must be replaced with an entirely new certificate (and not just changed). A trust reset is only required in the case the number of compromised  keys at the same time is greater or equal than the TRC's quorum (see [](#quorum)).
```

@matzf so there is no revocation for CA certificates?  If one of it is compromised, do you just have to wait that it rolls over (typically 11 days)? Shall we add something more on recovery from compromise? 

In addition, we don't talk about cert revocation at all, if not in the security considerations.. Does that sound good?

> It would be good to mention that the CA certificates are not revoked, but short lived.


Done in https://github.com/scionassociation/scion-cppki_I-D/pull/25/commits/41bc0a15cb19372761910aa2fa620f7b27cccbdc
Sounds good to me.

Correct, there is no revocation, only expiration or TRC updates.
For a CA compromise, the alternative to waiting for expiration is to change the root key by updating the TRC. There is still a large-ish window for attacks during the time it takes for the TRC to be distributed and the grace period. This should be comparably shorter than expiration (I'd assume something on the order of dozens of minutes to few hours at most).

To me, only discussing (the lack of) revocations in the security considerations section does indeed not seem ideal. This is a big design decision and should be discussed front and center.

The book has a short and clearly written section on this topic (3.1.10 Substitutes to Certificate Revocation) that we could adapt. This would fit well into the section  "Trust Relations within an Isolation Domain".

While the wording is clearer now, I think this is moving away from the point that it was trying to make (or that I thought it should be making); that the PKI messages needed to verify the control plane messages don't pose any additional DoS risk.

I've also added the point about certificate renewal being a point of failure. 

```suggestion
The relying party must be able to discover and obtain new or updated cryptographic material. For the control plane messages, this is simplified by the observation that the sender of a message (e.g. of a path construction beacon during path exploration or a path segment during a path lookup) always has all the cryptographic material to verify it. Thus, the receiver can always immediately obtain all the cryptographic material from the message originator.
As the corresponding PKI messaging thus only occurs when the control plane is already communicating, these requests to obtain cryptographic material are not prone to additional denial of service attacks. We therefore refer to {{I-D.scion-cp}} for a more detailed description of DoS vulnerabilities of control-plane messages.

For certificate renewal, on the other hand, this does not apply.
Denial of Service on the CA infrastructure or on the communication links from the individual ASes to the CA, could be used by an attacker to refuse victim ASes from  renewing their certificates. After the short-lived certificates expire, an AS is effectively banished from the network.
To address this risk, the individual isolation domains and/or CAs should have policies and processes to renew certificates out-of-band.
```
Thank you for rewording! I'm not fully convinced by OOB CA communication. Let's discuss

Thus, there is a need for a trust architecture that supports meaningful trust roots in a global environment with inherently distrustful parties. This new trust architecture should provide the following properties:

```suggestion
The monopoly model suffers from two main drawbacks: First, all parties must agree on a single root of trust. Secondly, the single root of trust represents a single point of failure, the misuse of which enables the forging of certificates. Also, its revocation can result in a kill-switch for all the entities it certifies. The oligopoly model relies on several roots of trust, all equally and completely trusted. However, this is not automatically better: Whereas the monopoly model has a single point of failure, the oligopoly model has the drawback of exposing more than one point of failure.
```

```suggestion
To fulfill the above requirements, which in fact apply well to inter-domain networking, SCION introduces the concept of **Isolation Domains**. An Isolation Domain (ISD) is a building block to support heterogeneous trust while achieving high availability and scalability in {{I-D.dekater-scion-controlplane}}. It consists of a logical grouping of SCION ASes that share a uniform trust environment (i.e. a common jurisdiction).
```

```suggestion
Authentication in SCION is based on X.509 certificates that bind identifiers to public keys and carry digital signatures that are verified by roots of trust. SCION allows each ISD to define its own set of trust roots, along with the policy governing their use. Such scoping of trust roots within an ISD improves security as compromise of a private key associated with a trust root cannot be used to forge a certificate outside the ISD. An ISD's trust roots and policy are encoded in the TRC, which has a version number, a list of public keys that serves as root of trust for various purposes, and a voting quorum governing the number of signatures required to update TRCs. The TRC serves as a way to bootstrap all authentication within SCION. Additionally, TRC versioning is used to efficiently revoke compromised roots of trust.
```

```suggestion
- **Core ASes**: They are a distinct set of ASes in the SCION Control Plane. For each ISD, the core ASes are listed in the TRC and each core AS has links to the other core ASes (in the same or in different ISDs).
```

```suggestion
- Check that all signatures are verifiable and no superfluous signatures are attached.
```

```suggestion
- **Voting ASes**: They may sign TRC updates. The process of appending a signature to a new TRC is called "casting a vote", and the designated ASes that hold the private keys to sign a TRC update are "voting ASes".
```

pulls.title: Add asn.1 module for TRCs and certificate extensions
pulls.author: nicorusti
pulls.body:
Resolves #28 

Also resolves: #125 

This PR contains: 
- RFC5280 ASN.1 module copied from [here](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/erlang/otp/master/lib/asn1/test/asn1_SUITE_data/PKIX1Explicit88.asn1), required for decoding X.509 certificates. There is one trick in this file:  
`CertificateSerialNumber`  was changed from  `INTEGER`  to  `[UNIVERSAL 2] IMPLICIT OCTET STRING` to avoid an integer overflow problem in tooling, since serial numbers are 20-21 bytes long. 
- SCION TRC ASN.1 module  

This module was used to successfully decode various production TRCs using asn1c and OSS Noklava ASN.1 studio

[Diff with main](https://author-tools.ietf.org/api/iddiff?url_1=https://scionassociation.github.io/scion-cppki_I-D/draft-dekater-scion-pki.txt&url_2=https://scionassociation.github.io/scion-cppki_I-D/asn-1/draft-dekater-scion-pki.txt)

comments.author: nicorusti
comments.body:
I tried to build an ASN.1 module for TRCs based on what is in [docs](https://docs.scion.org/en/latest/cryptography/trc.html#trc-payload-schema) and in the draft already, then use it to  verify that the SSFN & SSUN TRCs can somehow be verified. I used code generated by the ASN.1 compiler [asn1c](http://lionet.info/asn1c/asn1c-usage.html). I think what is on docs is outdated and that there are some differences.


This is what I did:

1. Compile the ASN.1 module (note that I also had to feed [PKIX1Explicit88](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/erlang/otp/master/lib/asn1/test/asn1_SUITE_data/PKIX1Explicit88.asn1) containing ASN.1 modules from ~RFC3280~ RFC5280
```
asn1c -pdu=TRCPayload ../PKIX1Explicit88.asn1 ../trc.asn1
make -f Makefile.am.sample
```

2. In the TRC, find the offset where CMS data is: Look at the output for an OCTET STRING that appears shortly after the pkcs7-data object identifier. It will have an offset number on the far left (for example, 60).
```
sed 's/BEGIN TRC/BEGIN CMS/g; s/END TRC/END CMS/g' ISD70-fd4b0d2abe1b4c54274f46eeb31d66cb.bundle| openssl asn1parse -inform PEM
```
3. Extract the payload using that offset 
```
sed 's/BEGIN TRC/BEGIN CMS/g; s/END TRC/END CMS/g' SD76-495dd7abbc765925299032679fc61b79.bundle | openssl asn1parse -inform PEM -strparse 60 -out ssun_trc.der -noout
```
4. Check if the compiled ASN.1 module can verify the TRC content. Here I get stuck, likely because the ASN.1 module does not match the TRC
```
./progname -d  -iber -otext ../ssun_trc.der
AD: Processing ../ssun_trc.der
AD: Decoding 8192 bytes
AD: decode(0) consumed 65+0b (8192), code 2
AD: Clean up partially decoded structure
AD: ofp 1, no=65, oo=0, dbl=0
../ssun_trc.der: Decode failed past byte 65: Input processing error
```

Looking at the TRC content, I see a few things that maybe don't match the ASN.1 module (see comments). I got as far as this, I need support from someone more familiar with this.

comments.author: nicorusti
comments.body: JFYI, with the current ASN module, I was able to decode all initial TRCs available [here](https://learn.anapaya.net/docs/resources/assignments/isds/). I extracted the TRC content using: ``` for f in *.bundle; do [ -e "$f" ] || continue; base="${f%.bundle}"; echo "Processing $f -> ${base}.der..."; sed 's/BEGIN TRC/BEGIN CMS/g; s/END TRC/END CMS/g' "$f" | openssl asn1parse -inform PEM -strparse 60 -out "${base}.der" -noout; done ``` I could then parse all of them them both with asn1c (see [decoded_trcs.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26636062/decoded_trcs.txt) ) and with ASN.1 Studio. With the latter tool, I could also verify all subsequent TRCs for ISD 70 and 71: Extract subsequent TRCs: ``` awk ' /-----BEGIN TRC-----/ { count++; file="ISD70_trc_" count ".pem" } { if(file) print > file } /-----END TRC-----/ { close(file); file="" } ' ISD70-fd4b0d2abe1b4c54274f46eeb31d66cb.bundle ``` Convert them to .der ``` for f in *.pem; do [ -e "$f" ] || continue; base="${f%.bundle}"; echo "Processing $f -> ${base}.der..."; sed 's/BEGIN TRC/BEGIN CMS/g; s/END TRC/END CMS/g' "$f" | openssl asn1parse -inform PEM -strparse 60 -out "${base}.der" -noout; done ``` They look valid in ASN.1 studio Overall, all TRCs are valid according to the current module version, except for ISD72, whose description field is too long.

We need to drop the DEFAULT FALSE, otherwise all current TRCs are invalid according to DER encoding (which prohibits including the value if it matches the default value.)

So is it a requirement (MUST) that validity is always encoded as `GeneralizedTime`? If yes, we also need to update the draft text, which now only says:
```
   *  validity field: Defines the validity period of the certificate.

      -  *SCION constraints*: All certificates MUST have a well-defined
         expiration date.  GeneralizedTime value "99991231235959Z" MUST
         not be used.

      -  *Additional conditions and remarks*: SCION recommends a
         specific maximum validity period for each type of certificate.
         For details, see Section 2.1.6.  SCION implementations SHOULD
         adopt these values.
 ```

If that is the case, then I don't think the problem is importing `Validity`, rather  importing `Time`: according to [rfc3280 section 4.1.2.5](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3280.html#section-4.1.2.5):
> CAs conforming to this profile MUST always encode certificate
   validity dates through the year 2049 as UTCTime; certificate validity
   dates in 2050 or later MUST be encoded as GeneralizedTime.
   
The ASN.1 module in the RFC is: 
```
Validity ::= SEQUENCE {
     notBefore      Time,
     notAfter       Time  }

Time ::= CHOICE {
     utcTime        UTCTime,
     generalTime    GeneralizedTime }
```

If we import Time, we are allowing both UTCTime and GeneralizedTime. What do you think the best solution is? 

a)  Not import any of those, and have our own definitions? (but are there any interop drawbacks with existing libraries)?

That section talks about certificates not TRC payload. We cannot change that definition given we need to be compliant with RFC5280.

> > CAs conforming to this profile MUST always encode certificate
> > validity dates through the year 2049 as UTCTime; certificate validity
> > dates in 2050 or later MUST be encoded as GeneralizedTime.

The problem is importing `Validity` as it does not match the definition of what you suggest to import.
(Of course it needs to be redefined with `GeneralizedTime` and not `Time`, which we wrongly do on docs.scion.org. I guess that is what you are referring to)

> 
> If we have doubts we could consult our ISE reviewer, who is also the first author of rfc3280..

IMO, we should: Define `Validity` ourselves with only `GeneralizedTime`
`UTCTime` is avoided in many of the newer RFCs.

```
 Validity ::= SEQUENCE {
      notBefore      GeneralizedTime,
      notAfter       GeneralizedTime  }
```

> P.P.S.: I can successfully parse the trc using imports for both, so maybe option b) is a viable one?

If we do that, all code needs to respect that. We must be very sure that we do not create TRCs with UTCTime, before all nodes in the network have been upgraded to also support UTCTime. I don't see a real advantage of doing that, as UTCTime is not really a good choice in the first place.

Some examples of GeneralizedTime only:

- https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc3161#section-2.4.2
- https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc5755#section-4.2.6
- https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc4059#section-2.1

@oncilla I found the culprit: in the SSUN TRC, several `CertificateSerialNumber` are 20-21 bytes integers.  
This field was indeed defined in PKIX1Explicit88  as an INTEGER. However, I think asn1c was getting stuck due to an integer overflow. Changing PKIX1Explicit88 from 
`CertificateSerialNumber  ::=  INTEGER `
to 
`CertificateSerialNumber  ::=  [UNIVERSAL 2] IMPLICIT OCTET STRING`
Makes asn1c avoid decoding this long integer, with this trick it correctly decode the TRC using the current ASN.1 module

Or if we do, maybe something like uint16?

> I wonder though if 255 is the right value.

```

Let's go with scion PEN first, and ask if there is something other we should use.

So far, we didn't check the length, which was an oversight.
I think 1024 characters is too little, as is proven by the SSUN TRC payload already.

I think we would want a reasonable upper limit. Maybe 4096 or 8192 to be on the safe side?

We should also enforce this going forward during TRC creation.
Sure let's add it. (Not in the extKeyUsage attributes though)
Let's use EXPLICIT. (Agreed that it doesn't do anything now, but in case we extend the file, it is probably good to already have it.)

Ok let's make it 8192.. Are there any constraints with a large TRC? 
AFAIK this is always transmitted over gRPC in -controlplane, so there should not be size constraints
@oncilla let me know if you want to leave the current 255 limit, or if we should remove the limit overall
The message size limit for gRPC is usually ~4MB. So that is a theoretical upper limit for the TRC.

Let's change to safe upper bounds. I think that is way more than is reasonable, but it will ensure there is no unbounded integers (which is best practice AFAIU.)

```
votes SEQUENCE SIZE (0..32767) OF INTEGER (0..65535)
...
certificates       SEQUENCE SIZE (0..65535) OF Certificate
```

(32767 because at most half can vote (regular vs sensitive) and 1 root is required)


If you want to restrict it further, we can try to choose sensible values based on the 4MB upper limit.
I think that gives you around 4k-5k certificates tops.
Good, leaving 8192 bytes as we are far away from the gRPC limit.

I think that gives you around 4k-5k certificates tops.

@oncilla how about this: 

Note that I think we also have to adjust the quorum accordingly!

       coreASes           SEQUENCE OF ASN,

```

Garcia Prado (ETH Zurich), Russ Housley (IETF), Brian Trammell (Google), Ramon Keller (LibC Technologies), Patrick Ambord (independent), and Kevin Meynell (SCION Association) for reviewing this document. We are also very grateful to Adrian Perrig (ETH Zurich), for providing guidance and feedback about each aspect of SCION. Finally, we are indebted to the SCION development teams of Anapaya and ETH Zurich, for their practical knowledge and for the documentation about the CP PKI, as well as to the authors of {{CHUAT22}} - the book is an important source of input and inspiration for this draft.
```

```suggestion
In X.509 terms, Control Plane root certificates are CA certificates. For simplicity, this document calls them 'root certificates', distinguishing them from from the subordinate 'issuing CA certificates'. Root certificates are self-signed; the issuer and subject are the same entity, and the public key within the certificate is used to verify its own signature. The public key of the Control Plane root certificate and proof of ownership of the private key are embedded in the TRC of an ISD, via the self-signed Control Plane root certificate. This facilitates the bootstrapping of trust within an ISD, and marks the Control Plane root certificates as the starting point of an ISD's certificate verification path.
```

```suggestion
To validate a certification path, a relying party builds a collection of root certificates known as the trust anchor pool. ```suggestion

> In issuing CA certificates, the presence of this attribute ensures that the Control Plane can identify from which AS to retrieve the intermediate CA certificate, thereby avoiding circular dependencies.

reviews.author: nicorusti
reviews.body: @oncilla could you look at some of my comments, and eventually at the overall diff?

comments.body:
> if these attributes are set and support for them is missing, implementations SHOULD error out. 

What does "error out" mean? Reject the certificate? If so, this clashes with the  earlier MAY: an implementation would reject a cert with an attribute that we say is optional.

P.S.: This wording was introduced in a very early [draft version](https://github.com/scionassociation/scion-cppki_I-D/commit/6db6cc71211767996eac23814968bef0d3368d03#diff-46a3cbbb8fca7cc808ffd598a474834aeef4be1c1364b16a354475c27c499dacL603) and it comes straight from [docs](https://docs.scion.org/en/latest/cryptography/certificates.html#authority-key-identifier-extension), I believe we should revise it.

As far as I understand, using `authorityCertIssuer` & `authorityCertSerialNumber` is not supported in scionproto. If I'm right, then the old wording was incorrect. I therefore reworded to mention that implementations MUST only use `keyIdentifier` 
Correct @oncilla? 

I realized this section was weird because of this old wording:

> if these attributes are set and support for them is missing, implementations SHOULD error out. 

What does "error out" mean? Reject the certificate? If so, this clashes with the  earlier MAY: an implementation would reject a cert with an attribute that we say is optional.

P.S.: This wording was introduced in a very early [draft version](https://github.com/scionassociation/scion-cppki_I-D/commit/6db6cc71211767996eac23814968bef0d3368d03#diff-46a3cbbb8fca7cc808ffd598a474834aeef4be1c1364b16a354475c27c499dacL603) and it comes straight from [docs](https://docs.scion.org/en/latest/cryptography/certificates.html#authority-key-identifier-extension), this is why I think we have to change wording.

What is a voting-only certificate?  And these two sentences seem conflicting:
> Voting AS and issuing CA certificates MUST include the `ISD-AS number` attribute

> Voting-only certificates are not required to include the `ISD-AS number` attribute 

The first sentence was introduced [here](https://github.com/scionassociation/scion-cppki_I-D/commit/a4fa4628a2b9eadbc0e88053431dc884c0ad86a6), specifically in https://github.com/scionassociation/scion-cppki_I-D/pull/108/changes/b4624299438c05c18c86e5f6748738691acf7e56
This topic was discussed in [Slack](https://scionproto.slack.com/archives/C8ADA9CEP/p1740057439410749)
I reworded this section following Dominik's clarification.

This sentence is meant to explain the reason why you MUST include the ISD-AS attribute sometimes. The reason is to let relying parties discover where to go and fetch the right certificate (therefore avoiding circular dependencies with cryptography). I agree that the old wording is weird. How about something like: 
```suggestion
The presence of this attribute allows relying parties to identify the AS that operates the issuing CA, facilitating the discovery and retrieval of the intermediate CA certificate during chain validation.
```

Let me know what you think @oncilla. I don't think this is absolutely necessary, so if you  are still not convinced, I'll drop the sentence.
Ok changed to 8 days [f52cbdb](https://github.com/scionassociation/scion-cppki_I-D/pull/135/commits/f52cbdb3fff6e46cf86d3363a3b67bb092c04d49)
I feel this was not clear in the earlier text.  @oncilla Looks good?

I don't think a comma is needed here. 

The public keys of Voting AS certificates must therefore be explicitly verified during the Signing Ceremony ([](#initial-ceremony)) that is used to bootstrap trust for the initial TRC.

```suggestion
All certificates used in the Control Plane PKI are in X.509 v3 format {{RFC5280}} and additionally the TRC contains self-signed certificates instead of plain public keys. Self-signed certificates have the following advantages over plain public keys: (1) They make the binding between name and public key explicit; and (2) the binding is signed to prove possession of the corresponding private key. The public keys of Voting AS certificates must therefore be explicitly verified during the Signing Ceremony ([](#initial-ceremony)) that is used to bootstrap trust for the initial TRC.
```
